channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: NASA names 4 astronauts on the 'highly complex' Artemis III lunar training mission
item.description: The crew of four — NASA astronaut and commander Randy Bresnik, European Space Agency pilot Luca Parmitano, NASA mission specialists Frank Rubio and Andre Douglas — are scheduled to launch next year.
item.content:encoded:
The crew of four — NASA astronaut and commander Randy Bresnik, European Space Agency pilot Luca Parmitano, NASA mission specialists Frank Rubio and Andre Douglas — are scheduled to launch next year.

(Image credit: Ronaldo Schemidt)
item.dc:creator: Brendan Byrne

item.title: Whales are showing up in San Francisco Bay. New ship alerts could help protect them
item.description: The changing climate is driving whales into San Francisco Bay, where ship strikes have been deadly. A new camera system could help ships and ferries steer clear.
item.content:encoded:
The changing climate is driving whales into San Francisco Bay, where ship strikes have been deadly. A new camera system could help ships and ferries steer clear.

(Image credit: Darrin Allen)
item.dc:creator: Lauren Sommer

item.title: Can birds outsing traffic? Some are trying
item.description: New research from scientists at the Centre for Ecological Research in Hungary finds that some birds living in cities are changing their songs to compete with traffic and other urban noise.
item.content:encoded: New research from scientists at the Centre for Ecological Research in Hungary finds that some birds living in cities are changing their songs to compete with traffic and other urban noise.
item.dc:creator: Adrian Florido

item.title: Look up! Venus and Jupiter are sharing a 'cosmic kiss'
item.description: Jupiter and Venus will appear close together in the sky Monday and Tuesday in what astronomy calls a "conjunction."
item.content:encoded: Jupiter and Venus will appear close together in the sky Monday and Tuesday in what astronomy calls a "conjunction."
item.dc:creator: Ayesha Rascoe

item.title: It's one of the world's most isolated islands. Here come the bulldozers
item.description: The Indian government is spending $9 billion to create a megaport, airport and city on this remote island. Critics fear the impact on pristine forests and the lives of indigenous inhabitants.
item.content:encoded:
The Indian government is spending $9 billion to create a megaport, airport and city on this remote island. Critics fear the impact on pristine forests and the lives of indigenous inhabitants.

(Image credit: Omkar Khandekar/NPR)
item.dc:creator: Omkar Khandekar

item.title: Bumblebees have tiny brains but they can solve problems like chimps and elephants
item.description: New research suggests the fuzzy insects may be capable of spontaneously solving problems the way animals with much larger brains do.
item.content:encoded:
New research suggests the fuzzy insects may be capable of spontaneously solving problems the way animals with much larger brains do.

(Image credit: Mikko Törmänen/University of Oulu)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: Immunologist Nicole Baumgarth explains why ticks are spreading to new regions
item.description: NPR's Eyder Peralta speaks with immunologist Nicole Baumgarth about why ticks are spreading to new regions, and what this increase in ticks could mean for the spread of Lyme disease.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Eyder Peralta speaks with immunologist Nicole Baumgarth about why ticks are spreading to new regions, and what this increase in ticks could mean for the spread of Lyme disease.
item.dc:creator: Eyder Peralta

item.title: What teeth enamel tells us about ancient human diets
item.description: The enamel on our teeth is the hardest tissue on the human body. A new study looks at the nanoscale structure of enamel from teeth dating as far back as 18 million years ago to see how it's changed.
item.content:encoded: The enamel on our teeth is the hardest tissue on the human body. A new study looks at the nanoscale structure of enamel from teeth dating as far back as 18 million years ago to see how it's changed.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Rott

item.title: Scientists in 'autonomous laboratories' are starting to outsource work to robots
item.description: Scientists are building autonomous robotic labs powered by artificial intelligence. The goal, they say, is for these robots to take over human researchers' most laborious, time-consuming tasks.
item.content:encoded:
Scientists are building autonomous robotic labs powered by artificial intelligence. The goal, they say, is for these robots to take over human researchers' most laborious, time-consuming tasks.

(Image credit: Jodi Hilton for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Katia Riddle

item.title: Scientists probe how pigeons use magnetism to navigate
item.description: Homing pigeons rely on a variety of signals to navigate, including magnetism. But it hasn't been clear how they detect magnetic cues. Researchers propose the answer may be found in the birds' livers.
item.content:encoded: Homing pigeons rely on a variety of signals to navigate, including magnetism. But it hasn't been clear how they detect magnetic cues. Researchers propose the answer may be found in the birds' livers.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel
